data.title: MercadoLibre: Brazil Hypergrowth Justifies The Premium
data.description:
With the S&P 500 racing ahead to new all-time highs, powered by a return to "risk on" sentiment and a rebound in tech stocks, many investors are asking, "What pockets of the stock market are still buying opportunities, especially for investors who are cautious on valuations?

I continue to find international e-commerce plays like Sea (SE) and MercadoLibre (MELI) to be very opportunistic buys, supported by their secular emerging-market tailwinds and rising profit. MercadoLibre is down ~10% since the start of the year and roughly ~20% from highs near $2,300 notched just earlier this year in February, technically putting the stock in a bear market. The question for investors now is, will the pain continue, or is it time to buy the dip?

I last wrote a buy article on MercadoLibre in January, when the stock was trading at ~$2,100 per share. While my buy call was too premature, my conviction in the company has only increased after its recent decline in share prices, giving us an even lower, more de-risked entry point in a fiercely growing business. We are especially encouraged by MercadoLibre's strong GMV growth over the holiday quarter, which helps to demonstrate that the company's secular tailwinds are powering past global macroeconomic consumer malaise. I reiterate my buy rating here.

To me, these are the key reasons to be long on MercadoLibre:

Massive GMV growth rates in Brazil and Mexico

MercadoLibre is achieving >30% y/y GMV growth in its two largest markets, which are Brazil and Mexico. Though MercadoLibre is already the dominant e-commerce giant of Latin America, it is exhibiting growth rates that are closer to a startup.

Secular emerging market tailwinds and two highly attractive revenue streams

MercadoLibre operates in some of the fastest-growing economies in the world, including and especially Brazil, a member of the so-called "BRICS" countries. The company isn't just an e-commerce brand, but its fintech arm, MercadoPago, is also aiming to be the financial services titan of the region, winning in both payments and lending.

Healthy credit metrics

Even amid a potentially souring global macro, MercadoLibre's non-performing loan rates are declining, while net interest margins are showing sequential improvements. It has a strong balance sheet that is over-provisioned for bad debt.

Now, there's a premium to consider here in MercadoLibre's valuation. MercadoLibre trades at a 19x forward adjusted EBITDA, which is well above Amazon (AMZN) at 13x and Singapore's Sea (SE) at ~11x, which is the cheapest stock in the pack (and also my highest-conviction buy in e-commerce).

Yet we should also consider the fact that MercadoLibre is still in hyper-growth territory. Yes, the company is investing a bit of its margin in order to spur GMV growth, particularly in Brazil (which is one of the key concerns driving this stock at the moment). But with huge markets to address in Latin America plus a history of strong execution, we think the company's near-40% GMV growth makes near-term multiples for this stock less meaningful. We like the fact that we have an opportunity to buy MercadoLibre at a discount relative to where it traded in the past, at a mid-20s or even low-30s multiple.

Use the dip as a buying opportunity.

Q4 download

Let's now go through MercadoLibre's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

MercadoLibre's revenue surged 45% y/y to $8.76 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $8.47 billion (+40% y/y) by a wide five-point margin. The e-commerce division recorded $4.98 billion of revenue, up 40% y/y and representing 57% of the company's total, while fintech (MercadoPago) drove 51% y/y revenue growth to $3.78 billion in revenue, primarily buoyed by the company's expanding loan book and favorable net interest income driving a 78% y/y surge in credit revenue to $1.85 billion.

Underlying e-commerce activity in the quarter was incredibly strong, which defied worries of a global spending slowdown ahead of the holiday season. The chart below showcases the company's GMV growth (total dollar sales on the platform) by key market, adjusted for FX swings. Brazil grew 35% y/y, accelerating one point versus Q3 and the best growth rate all year, while Mexico showcased the same 35% y/y growth rate and also posted its best quarter of the year. Overall, MercadoLibre achieved 37% FX-neutral GMV growth.

Key to the company's GMV growth has been increased order frequency by a more loyal user base. The company added 6.4 million net-new buyers in the quarter to end at a whopping 83.2 million active buyers (+24% y/y). But key to this as well has been the fact that each buyer now orders an average of 9 items per quarter, up from 7.8 in the year-ago quarter.

Importantly, the company has reduced the free shipping threshold in Brazil to R$19 ($3.80), which has buoyed GMV growth and order frequency in MercadoLibre's largest market. Per CFO Martin de los Santos' remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

In Brazil, our largest market, GMV grew an impressive 35% year-over-year alongside a 45% increase in sold items. This acceleration is the result of our strategic investments to enhance the value proposition, most notably the decision to lower the free shipping threshold. More free shipping is driving higher purchase frequency and bringing new buyers into the ecosystem. This volume is translating directly into efficiency. Our logistics network absorbed the increase in volumes while driving productivity gains, proving our ability to scale effectively. Our value proposition is generating traction in Mexico, too, where GMV also grew 35%."

On the credit side, we are monitoring mixed news on the credit portfolio. The bright side is that the percentage of non-performing loans that are past 90 days due fell -70 bps y/y to 16.8%. That said, the percentage of loans that are between 15 and 90 days past due crept up 40 bps y/y to 7.6%.

We still note that the total NPL percentage improved 30 bps y/y. This is impressive amid MercadoPago's rising credit portfolio, which grew 90% y/y to $12.51 billion, indicating that the company isn't necessarily sacrificing credit quality in order to grow its loan book.

Also, we note that balance sheet provisions sit at 150% of currently designated NPLs for loans past 90 days due and 103% for loans past 15 days due. The company has been increasing its provision coverage in a conservative manner.

Of course, one of the core issues with the stock right now is a pullback in operating margins, partially due to rising bad debt/provision coverage. EBIT margins fell -240 bps y/y to 10.1%. This was additionally pulled down by a -220 bps y/y headwind in COGS (due in part to the lower free shipping threshold in Brazil leading to higher logistics costs as a percentage of revenue), offset by opex leverage in G&A and product development.

In spite of margin headwinds, we still note that nominal profitability rose at a healthy double-digit pace. Adjusted EBITDA grew 16% y/y to $1.13 billion.

Q1 look-ahead, risks, and key takeaways

As the market shifts its attention to MercadoLibre's upcoming Q1 earnings print (due on May 7), there's a lot of focus on the potential negative catalysts, particularly around margins. Here are the key risks that investors are watching ahead of the earnings print:

  • Credit metrics. Rising provision coverage has dealt a blow to the company's EBIT margins over the past few quarters. A continued deterioration in non-performing loan ratios may require the company to step up bad debt provisions, but at the same time, if the recent trend of improvements in the 90+ loan bucket hold, we may see more margin tailwinds here.
  • GMV trends in a tougher macro. So far, GMV held up strong through the holiday quarter for MercadoLibre. Investors are cautiously waiting to see if growth rates decelerate in Q1, given the rise in oil prices plus potential impacts from regime change in Venezuela.
  • Margin trends. A more competitive/discount-heavy retail environment, plus the ongoing logistics headwinds from lower free shipping thresholds in Brazil, may continue to put pressure on EBIT margins in Q1 and beyond.

That said, we think the stock's fall since the start of the year discounts the possibility of a positive update on these catalysts, and with the lower entry point now at a reasonable ~19x adjusted EBITDA multiple, I'm happy to stay long here.

data.title: A New Short Squeeze
data.description:
Originally published on April 15, 2026

Stocks have broadly moved higher this month, and of the Russell 1000 members, one stock has left the rest of the index far behind. Month to date, the single best-performing Russell 1000 member has been Avis Budget (CAR). The stock is up over 130% MTD, whereas the next best performer, Astera Labs (ALAB), is up by not even half as much (49%).

We noted the vehicle rental firm in a couple of X.com posts (here and here). The rally has been driven in part by a short squeeze, as the stock has more than half of its shares sold short, as we will detail further below.

In a post on Monday, we highlighted how the S&P 500 recently snapped a seven-day winning streak while several individual members of the index have gone on historic winning streaks in their own respects.

Given its monumental run, CAR can also be added to the list of winning streaks. As shown below, assuming it closes higher on Tuesday, it would be its tenth consecutive daily gain.

Again, the rally in CAR is, in all likelihood, a short squeeze. Short interest data as of the end of March was published last Friday, and those readings showed 54% of the float in CAR sold short. On that basis, it is the most heavily bet against stock in the Russell 1000.

Additionally, CAR appears to be the only heavily shorted name getting squeezed. In the table below, we show the 25 stocks with the most short interest in the Russell 1000, and while a small majority are higher MTD, none are even close to the rally in CAR, nor is such a large share of float shorted.

For example, Fermi (FRMI) started the year with a reading above 70%. That share is down to 46.6% as of the most recent data, as the stock has fallen 32.7% since the start of the year.

Below, we show average short interest readings for all members of each industry group in the Russell 1000. Due to CAR, the transportation industry is the fifth most heavily shorted group, although if that stock were removed, the industry would rank in the middle of the pack.

A related industry is in a similar boat. The automobile and components industry ranks as the highest average short interest level at 9.6%. However, this is again due to some members being much more heavily shorted than others, namely EV-focused stocks.

Whereas legacy auto OEM and parts retailers have low to mid-single-digit short interest levels, EV stocks have readings in the mid-teens and upwards of 30% for the most heavily shorted name: Lucid (LCID). LCID is also the third most heavily shorted stock of all Russell 1000 members.

Additionally, we would note that these EV names do have one exception. Tesla (TSLA) is actually the least shorted stock in the industry, currently below 2% per the most recent print.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: NFIB Small Business Survey: Optimism Drops To 11-Month Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Originally published on April 15, 2026

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index fell 3.0 points to 95.8, dropping below the index's historical average for the first time since April 2025. This was below the forecast of 97.8 and puts the index at the 31st percentile of the series.

“The 20% Small Business Deduction and other supportive small business tax provisions in the Working Families Tax Cut Act have had many positives for small business owners,” said NFIB Chief Economist Bill Dunkelberg. “However, the dramatic spike in oil prices has spooked consumers and owners alike. Small business owners are having to absorb those higher input costs and pass them along to their customers.”

Key takeaways from March's report:

  • The decline was primarily driven by a sharp 11-point drop in positive profit trends and a 7-point decline in those expecting better business conditions
  • Rising Uncertainty and Inflation Concerns: The Uncertainty Index jumped to 92 (well above the average of 68), while inflation returned as a top-three concern. Owners are currently struggling to absorb and pass on higher input costs caused by a spike in oil prices.
  • Weakened Sales and Inventories: For the first time in four months, real sales gains turned negative. Consequently, inventory investment has hit its lowest level since May 2024, as owners scale back in anticipation of lower consumer demand.
  • Pullback on Capital Spending: Only 16% of owners plan to make capital outlays in the next six months. This is a 2-point decline from February and represents the lowest level of planned investment since November 2009.
  • Labor Market Moderation: While the Employment Index fell, labor quality remains a major hurdle. 32% of owners reported job openings they could not fill, and the net percent of owners planning to raise worker compensation dropped to its lowest level since July 2025.
  • Overall health of business evaluations: Excellent 13%, Good 51%, Fair 30%, Poor 4%

The first chart below tracks the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index since 1986, with a baseline level of 100. Notice the sharp declines in sentiment during the Great Financial Crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic. In contrast, the index showed relative resilience during the 2000-2003 Tech Bubble collapse.

Following the Great Recession, small-business sentiment remained weak for an extended period, a pattern that closely resembled the past two years of pandemic-related disruptions and high inflation. Another interesting pattern to the index was the nearly identical jumps following the 2016 and 2024 elections.

The average monthly change in the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is 1.4 points. To filter out short-term fluctuations, the chart below presents a 3-month moving average alongside the individual monthly values, represented by dots.

NFIB Small Business Survey Components

The NFIB Small Business Optimism Index is composed of ten components. In March, zero components increased, eight declined, and two were unchanged.

Business Optimism and Consumer Attitudes

The next few charts are overlays of the Business Optimism Index with two of the main measures of consumer attitudes: Conference Board Consumer Confidence and the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index.

The Consumer Confidence Index is influenced by employment and labor market conditions from the worker's perspective, whereas the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on employment conditions from the business perspective.

In our first chart comparing the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index, we can see that the consumer measure is the more volatile of the two. Therefore, it is plotted on a separate axis to give a better comparison of the two series from the common baseline of 100.

Next, we compare the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index with the University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Again, we've plotted each index on a separate axis; however, in this chart, we can see that the business measure is more volatile of the two.

Despite the volatility, though, we can see that these two measures of mood (business and consumer) have been highly correlated, falling and rising together for the most part. A decline in small business sentiment was a long leading indicator for the first two recessions of the century, but clearly not for the Covid-19 recession.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ESCO Technologies Gets A Downgrade, But Not Because Of This Transformational Move
data.description:
April 16th was a really interesting day for shareholders of ESCO Technologies Inc. (ESE). Management announced a major acquisition that will transform the business. Based on my own analysis of the deal, there is the potential that it could make the business even more attractive than it currently is. That is especially true in relation to cash flows. And it would occur even if synergies are not realized. It does ultimately increase the overall leverage situation of the business some. But on the whole, I would view this as a positive development.

Unfortunately, my one problem with the business is that it is anything but cheap. In fact, after surging 41.2% since I called it a Hold in September of last year, which dwarfed the 6.5% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time, I am inclined to downgrade it to a Sell.

A Major Development

On April 15th, the management team at ESCO Technologies announced the firm's decision to acquire Megger Group Limited, a privately held business that focuses on providing testing, monitoring, and data-driven services for utilities and critical electric infrastructure customers. Examples here include those in the industrial, transportation, data center, and renewable energy markets. This move will be transformative as it is rolled up into ESCO Technologies’ Utility Solutions Group segment. However, the overall impact on the business will be significant.

Before I get into the terms of the deal and the impact it will have on the firm, I would like to point you to the chart above. In it, you can see financial performance for ESCO Technologies over the last three fiscal years and for the first quarter of 2026 compared to the same time in 2025. The company has actually been growing at a rather nice pace.

And in each of the three charts below, you can see financial performance for its operating segments. The big drivers here have been its Utility Solutions Group segment and its Aerospace & Defense segment. This actually makes a great deal of sense to me.

Both of these are exposed to markets that are rather hot right now. The aviation industry has been particularly attractive following the end of the COVID-19 pandemic and a surge of investment by airlines in new aircraft. The U.S. government is spending more on its military budget than ever, and other countries are doing the same. And when it comes to the Utility Solutions Group, there is a clear catalyst when it comes to things like data centers, energy infrastructure, and more.

In light of all of this, it actually is perfectly logical that ESCO Technologies would acquire Megger. But this is not a small purchase. It is, in fact, quite large. According to the announcement, ESCO Technologies will be paying $2.35 billion for Megger. $900 million of this will be in cash, with the rest in the form of stock. Following the close of the transaction, the current owner of Megger, TBG, will own about 16% of the shares of the combined business.

For those who have never heard of Megger, it is worth noting that the company designs and manufactures electrical test and measurement equipment, with examples being diagnostic static motor testers, battery ground fault locators, insulation resistance testers, smart grid sensors, and more. This is a truly global enterprise, with only about 30% of revenue coming from North America. A solid 35% is attributable to Europe, while 15% comes from Asia. The rest comes from other miscellaneous nations across the planet.

Upon the close of this transaction, about 52% of revenue generated by ESCO Technologies will come from its Utility Solutions Group segment. That's up from 30% that was forecasted for 2026 prior to the deal being made. Exposure for the Aerospace & Defense segment will drop from 50% to 34%, while the Test segment will decline from 20% of sales to 14%. Keep in mind that the company has been making other changes to its portfolio. This included its divestiture last year of VACCO, marking the end of its exposure to the space market. It is highly likely that additional transactions will occur eventually. But in the meantime, management will be focused on capturing synergies from this purchase.

Over the course of three years, management expects annual run rate cost savings to be around $60 million. This will come from optimizing its combined footprint, specifically when it comes to its manufacturing and other related assets. It will also come from changes in sourcing strategy and cross-leveraging its engineering expertise. Management believes that there will be certain research and development synergies and efficiencies, not to mention go-to-market expenses and corporate overhead that can be optimized.

The $60 million in synergies is particularly tantalizing. For context, management mentioned that revenue generated by Megger should be around $590 million this year. Including the aforementioned synergies, management said that the cost of the transaction is 14 times EBITDA. That means that, without the synergies, we are looking at about $107.9 million in EBITDA that the company should generate. This actually makes it quite easy to see what the impact would be on ESCO Technologies.

In the table above, I calculated the net profits and operating cash flow from this transaction, both with synergies and without them. I assumed that the $900 million increase in net debt for the company will bear an annual interest rate of 6%. I also assumed a 21% tax rate and depreciation, depletion, and amortization that match what ESCO Technologies as a whole incurred in relation to revenue during 2025.

Using these estimates, as well as results for the business for 2025 and estimates for 2026, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. For this year, on an organic basis, management was expecting revenue of between $1.29 billion and $1.33 billion. From that, adjusted earnings per share had been forecast at between $7.90 and $8.15. At the midpoint, that would mean adjusted net profits of $207.7 million. Scaling up other profitability metrics similarly would give us adjusted operating cash flow of $253.6 million and EBITDA of $340 million.

The chart illustrates that, even without synergies, ESCO Technologies becomes cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis. This is because the company is quite literally using overpriced shares to pay for much of the deal. Because of the implied interest expense associated with the cash component, the stock does become more expensive on a price-to-earnings basis. But if synergies are ultimately realized, even on that basis, does it look cheaper? In the chart above, I compared the most relevant of these valuation scenarios to five other similar firms.

Unfortunately, on a price-to-earnings basis, four of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is. And when it came to the other valuation approaches, it was the most expensive of the group. So this is anything but a cheap prospect.

It is always possible that I could change my mind as new data comes out. And the good news is that management has provided some input regarding the future. They mentioned, for instance, that preliminary revenue for the second quarter of 2026 amounted to $309 million. That's up nicely from the $265.5 million the business reported for the second quarter of 2025. Earnings per share should be around $1.29, while adjusted earnings per share should be $1.91. This would mean net profits of $33.4 million and adjusted net profits of $49.4 million. For context, for the second quarter of 2025, the business generated net income of $31 million off of earnings per share of $1.20 and adjusted net profits of $34.9 million from $1.35 in adjusted earnings per share. But still, this is not enough, in my view, to make the company look cheap or even fairly valued. It is still, quite literally, a very pricey prospect.

Takeaway

Fundamentally speaking, I believe that this latest acquisition is a positive for the company. This is not to say that there aren't downsides. The rise in net debt for the business will push its net leverage ratio from 0.12 on a forward basis to around 2.5. But management is working to bring that down, with the goal of eventually lowering it to 2. As much as I love the overall trajectory that the company has been on and the path that it is taking moving forward, as expensive as it is, I think that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is the right move.

data.title: Mosaic: The Market Is Too Pessimistic Despite Iran-Driven Fertilizer Supply Disruptions
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered The Mosaic Company (MOS), I upgraded them to Buy, as the stock got cheaper despite fundamental improvements, cost savings, production increases and capital reallocation opportunities, with the market worrying about macro pressure while downplaying the company's recovery potential.

MOS is still a Buy, as the company remains strong, the management is navigating this environment well and the current price already seems to take into account significant levels of risk despite their near-term potential to benefit from the Iran conflict.

Internal Developments

MOS reported mixed Q4 results, with a miss on EPS despite a beat on revenue, while the FCF fell significantly to -$535 million in 2025 as a result of high CAPEX and a significant hit from working capital changes. This comes as a mix of the headwinds from higher sulfur prices and inventory build-up, which the company expects to stabilize in 2026 while these trends would revert and normalize.

For 2026, MOS expects phosphate production at or above 7 million tonnes and ~9 million tonnes of potash, with higher CAPEX of $1.5 billion, above the $1.36 billion seen in 2025, expecting this to fall to about $1 billion by 2030, which can unlock significant amounts of cash flow alongside projects like the one with Rainbow Rare Earths in Brazil in the long-term. For 2026, MOS sees an additional $100 million in further cost reductions in NA, supply chain efficiencies, with improved pricing, netbacks and margin optimizations.

Financially, based on MOS' latest report, we continue to see a strong position, with current assets above their current liabilities and a manageable amount of debt despite the net debt increasing by $829 million in 2025 to support their operations and CAPEX.

Mosaic also announced issuing $900 million worth of notes back in November 2025, with $500 million in 4.35% notes due January 2029 and $400 million of 4.60% ones due November 2030, which is very far from what a distressed company would see, further highlighting their quality compared to the stock market's valuation.

As a note, given their $7.53 billion market cap today, the company still trades at a PB of 0.62 even when excluding the non-controlling interests, which remains very attractive, especially since it's much higher than back in 2024 despite the increase in debt, with their initiatives mentioned below potentially allowing them to unlock some of this value.

Although the cash flow was negative due to the factors mentioned above, the company paid $280.4 million worth of dividends last year, with a yield standing at about 3.71% based on today's price.

Mosaic is expected to report its next earnings on May 1st, with the market estimating Normalized EPS of $0.21 and revenue of $2.84 billion. Given the macro volatility seen in recent months due especially to the Iran conflict, I'll pay close attention to their plans of monetizing their inventory build-up and other stakes, potentially even seeing them consider more asset sales such as Nutrien's (NTR) Profertil sale to Adecoagro (AGRO) not long ago, allowing them to monetize the current environment (which I believe will be temporary) while the stock doesn't show it. The company's CEO also hinted at something in this direction during their Q4 Earnings Call:

Looking ahead to 2026, we expect progress on multiple fronts. We're pursuing strategic alternatives for selected Brazilian assets, including unlocking incremental value from co-products, niobium and other critical minerals. We also expect to generate value through monetization of some of our Florida land holdings.

Iran Tailwinds Temporary - Yet Still Ignored

Right now, the ongoing Iran conflict will likely play the main role in the company's near-term future. This is something that goes well beyond just reaching peace there - with the IMF warning recently that we could even see a recession if that doesn't happen soon - as even a complete end today would not fix things due to the damage that's already been done to the region's infrastructure.

This is why it's really tough to see this a just a "temporary" increase in pump prices, as once the Strait of Hormuz is open, we'll likely have a mix of higher demand from countries seeking to replenish their strategic reserves combined with weaker supply given the infrastructure that will have to be repaired, with Qatar even stating over a month ago that the damage done on their Ras Laffan Industrial City can take as much as 3 to 5 years to repair, which can translate into a significant wave of inflation across the board.

Although the market is talking a lot about the implications on the price of oil since that's already visible at everyone's local gas station, we should also not forget the significant role this region plays in nitrogen-based fertilizers, sulfur, and ammonia, with the last two being key raw materials for Mosaic's own production.

The deputy executive director of the World Food Program also talked about this, highlighting how deep this conflict can turn out to be for the roots of the global economy, which is something that the market may not be accounting for just yet:

In the worst case, this means lower yields and crop failures next season. In the best case, higher input costs will be included in food prices next year.

As stated in the article, this conflict can restrict about 30% of global urea trade, while the region exports over 40% of the world's sulfur, which is a key byproduct of the oil and gas industry that plays a very important role for MOS as well.

However, given the significant rise in the company's inventory last year and the jump in nitrogen-based fertilizer prices (and the fall in supply due to shipping constraints) as a result of the Iran conflict, it wouldn't be impossible to see MOS benefit from more demand in the near-term, as we're now in the middle of the planting season and delays/higher shipping costs can push demand towards the company's production.

Still, this conflict also highlights the company's strategic importance, while a de-escalation and eventual normalization of the global economy despite the likely inflationary shock that can push the Fed into pausing rate cuts or even hiking - further slowing down the economy - can benefit Mosaic, which should soon finish its investment cycle and potentially capitalize on a recovery.

MOS was also highlighted as a preferred pick in the ongoing fertilizer rally by BofA not long ago, citing expectations of prices offsetting the costs and the factors mentioned above, all while the valuation remains attractive. A couple of days later however, the same institution downgraded MOS to Neutral from Buy, reducing their price rating to $30 from $33 per share, and citing the higher costs of sulfur and ammonia as dilutive to the company's earnings growth and cash flow.

However, there's also massive potential from their Saudi Arabian Ma'aden shares to be monetized, with an estimated value of $1.8 billion at the end of 2025 and $2.1 billion as of February 24, 2026 - perhaps even more following the Iran attacks - which can add a ton of financial flexibility for a company valued at ~$7.53 billion today, and that's on top of several other capital reallocation initiatives they highlight above. Note that a third of these shares are locked in until 2027, with the other two-thirds following in 2028 and 2029, so they're not able to sell it just yet.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, even though we're talking about vital commodities and a company that has potential to unlock significant value from their capital reallocation and investments, the stock and the business will always depend on the price of the commodity at least in the near-term, making volatility quite likely, which is why the focus should be on the long-term.

Although MOS seems cheap at current levels from several points of view, that's been the case for quite some time now, while the Iran conflict adds an extra layer of uncertainty during an already tight spot for the market.

Valuation

For MOS' DCF to Equity, assuming commodity prices similar to last year throughout the cycle and no change in net borrowings since the company's balance sheet remains solid, given the higher CAPEX expected in 2026 and a normalization in working capital/inventories, I'll assume a FCF of $300 million, followed by $500 million in 2027, $650 million in 2028, $750 million in 2029 and $900 million in 2030 as the CAPEX falls to $1 billion as they expect and they successfully monetize and invest their Ma'aden shares.

This would be followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years given the potential from other projects and investments (i.e. the one with Rainbow Rare Earths) and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and given the macro pressure and commodity-related risks, I'll assume a 10% discount rate to demand a solid return, with 317.4 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $10.21 billion, for an intrinsic value of $32.18 per share, which is well above the current $23.96 at the moment of writing this, indicating that the market is more conservative, while there's more potential especially if we see new buybacks.

Conclusion

MOS remains a Buy, backed by a strong business and good management that's able to navigate the current environment, standing to benefit from a near-term boost from the Iran conflict that's not recognized by the market yet.

There are certainly risks to keep in mind since we're talking about a commodity company, but the current price already seems to take into account a solid margin of safety, making the risk-reward attractive especially if the focus is on long-term.

data.title: Nvidia: I'm Finally Convinced (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Looking Back on Growth

I've changed my mind on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA). I last wrote about it a year ago. My main misgiving? I compared them to other companies with revenue in the hundreds of billions:

Difference here is they all get this amount of business by offering things that are real and not abstract.

Other megacaps have hundreds of billions in revenue because people need groceries, phones, and energy. Nvidia's value-add was still too abstract to me, and I doubted it. It didn't look doomed, but I couldn't see the reason to buy.

But now I don't have those doubts. I think it brings tangible value, and I want to get into that. First, I'm going to walk us back a bit. A few years ago, gaming was a key part of revenue. That changed fast.

Revenue shot up in a few years. Pretty much none of that was from gaming. No, it was data centers.

Last year that market pulled $193B in revenue. They basically created a whole new business out of thin air. You'll maybe understand that I didn't exactly expect that. Then I was skeptical that they could keep it up.

So then I had to ask how and why?

Pricing and Volume

GPUs changed. First they were just products. Now they're production itself.

Consumers buy gaming laptops. They'll only pay so much for that. Retail prices are in the hundreds of dollars. That was the limit beforehand.

GPU prices aren't public. We have to estimate them. The range is $10K to $50K per chip. Think about that. That's like Nvidia making entry-level hires.

Look at the biggest hyperscalers. The top ten have about 7.5M GPUs. Nvidia has like 92% of their market, though, so it's mostly their stuff. They spent billions to get these. That was Nvidia's growth.

But will they keep buying, or is there a ceiling?

Up, Up, and Up

The problem is, it's hard to tell how big this market is. It's too new. I mean, CEO Jensen Huang announced $1T worth of GPU orders through 2027. There's demand, but one thing will be different: AI inference. That's why Nvidia acquired Groq and invested in Intel (INTC) last year. Nvidia is prepping for inferencing.

Huang told us what's at stake in his last earnings call:

...compute is revenues. Without compute, there's no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there's no way to grow revenues. So in this new world of AI, compute equals revenues.

I didn't quite grasp this before. AI seemed like a pet project. Maybe it would get big later. But no, it was big right away. Hyperscalers are fighting for GPUs. Nations are too.

And then there are smuggling issues with China. And calls to halt exports there. The biggest businesses and the U.S. federal government want the product and don't want to share. Artificial intelligence is too precious.

What are the tangible value-adds that are so coveted?

There's their Blackwell product line. It's significantly cheaper for inference workloads. This was a big improvement in their H200 product. It also posed a big challenge to Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

Blackwell is good to scale. Vera Rubin is built for every step, from pre-training to inference. Vera Rubin is the bones of agentic AI. Financial institutions should find big use here. Agentic can support instant risk assessments and transaction approvals that take human agents much longer.

Then you have the Feynman GPU platform, which is scheduled for release in 2028. It won't just be a more efficient chip. It will be an efficiently packaged one, and that changes what a data center can do.

There's product variety, use cases, and no complacency. If there's a ceiling, I don't think we're about to hit it.

Good Value

NVDA traded at a big multiple before, and that deterred me. But management executed. Now I think they'll generate a lot of cash on these orders.

I'll start with the $1T. The most recent free cash flow margin was 44.8%. Basic math says that's $448B in FCF. Close to $200 per share, the market cap is $4.83T. That's a multiple around 11x. It seems to me like NVDA is priced to be a stalwart grower, to borrow a Peter Lynch term. I think that's a good deal, good value.

Risks

The main risk factor, in terms of returns, is the limits of GDP. The U.S. economy is $32T right now. Demand has to be global to support consistent revenue that reaches trillions.

Nvidia can't afford to have meaningful competitors either. Amazon (AMZN) has Trainium, their chance to compete on training. That's double-edged because Amazon has been a major customer until now. Other hyperscalers are working on their own in-house alternatives.

Then there are the strategic, geopolitical, and regulatory risks. Some lawmakers may view Nvidia as monopolistic and want to bust it up. Regulators may exert greater control on where they sell GPUs. Dependence on TSMC (TSM) for foundry services is one weak spot. That China could move aggressively on Taiwan is another, along with any other disruption in trade routes.

Better Late Than Never

Part of me wishes I had come around sooner, but I guess it's better late than never. Nvidia has proved what its product can do, and there's still more to be done.

data.title: NML: Data Centers Are A Catalyst But Flawed Portfolio Structure
data.description:
Overview

With growing concerns around the utility of AI, 2026 started with a rotation out of technology into other areas of the market. While the indices experienced choppiness, the Neuberger Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund Inc. (NML) was able to benefit from the market rotation. By focusing on energy and infrastructure, NML has a renewed growth catalyst to directly benefit from the growing power demands. When I previously covered NML, I issued a hold rating due to the underperformance against peers. However, I wanted to revisit the fund to take a more focused look at its value proposition, outlook, and dividend sustainability.

When I previously covered NML, the fund traded at a discount to NAV of 10.84%. Following the price rally in the first quarter of 2026, NML actually trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of 11.17%. However, valuation is a bit tricky because the fund has traded at an average discount to NAV of 14.51% over the last five-year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that NML currently remains at the higher end of its historical price-to-NAV range. However, the future growth catalyst of AI data centers can certainly cause the discount to NAV to shrink further.

The fund now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8.6% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. The fund did issue a recent dividend raise, but I worry about the negative impact of this. The fund may struggle to generate earnings that support distributions, leading to NAV erosion and limiting upside growth. So while the fund is aligned to participate in the expansion of AI, the underlying structure needs to be improved so that long-term performance is more attractive.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund fact sheet, NML has total managed assets of $665.9M that are spread across a diverse range of equities. As implied by the fund's name, it focuses specifically on investments that are primarily invested in companies that have some exposure to energy and infrastructure. More specifically, NML tends to lean more heavily on midstream natural resource companies. The fund takes an actively managed approach and doesn't try to replicate any specific index. What I like about NML is that it provides exposure to MLPs (Master Limited Partnerships) while shielding investors from the headache of schedule K-1 form. The portfolio's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Common Stocks: 68.4%.
  • Master Limited Partnerships: 31.3%.
  • Cash: 0.3%.

Approximately 69.1% of assets are allocated to midstream companies. This is followed by exposure to upstream and renewables, amounting to 26.1% and 3.1% of assets, respectively. By energy product segment, the fund is most heavily allocated towards natural gas and crude oil investments. This concentrated approach can work in the fund's favor during favorable market conditions but can also be a vulnerability during a downturn. Since the fund has no exposure to non-energy-related investments, NML will not display much of a correlation with traditional market indices.

A key part of the fund's strategy is the use of leverage in an effort to amplify returns. The fund currently has effective leverage totaling 21.85% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$174.4M. I consider this to be a moderate use of leverage, but it can still increase the overall risk profile for the fund. For instance, it is possible for leverage to be allocated to securities that decline in value, which would then amplify the fund's losses. Furthermore, leverage is riskier in this high-interest-rate environment. A higher cost of debt means that NML's spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities becomes thinner.

When looking at the top ten holdings, we can see that NML provides direct exposure to high-quality businesses. Admittedly, the fund takes a very focused approach and leans heavily on these top positions. The top ten holdings make up nearly 70% of total assets. For instance, the largest position is currently Targa Resources Corp. (TRGP) at 10.54% of assets. This is followed by many other notable positions, including DT Midstream (DTM), Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), and the Williams Companies (WMB), just to name a few.

Performance Insights

Since my last coverage, NML has released an updated annual report for 2025 that provides us with transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income landed at $0.02 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.07 per share. Net investment income is generated from the dividends and interest collected from NML's holdings. For a fund that prioritizes a high yield for investors, I am quite disappointed with the historical range for net investment income. The lackluster net investment income levels could be an indication that the fund's operating expenses are high.

The current structure leaves the fund very dependent on capturing net realized gains to support distributions and NAV growth. The greatest issue for this is that it leaves NML's share price and NAV vulnerable to market declines or periods where management cannot capture gains. Furthermore, it also means that management has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions to pay out the dividend, which steals from future growth and doesn't allow positions to grow uninterrupted over time. The end result is sporadic NAV growth that is hard to estimate year-over-year. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $6.32 per share.
  • 2022: $8.73 per share.
  • 2023: $8.02 per share.
  • 2024: $10.33 per share.
  • 2025: $9.72 per share.

So when measuring the long-term performance of NML, we can see how this dynamic shifts results for investors. Over the last ten years, NML's share price has increased by 44.6%. However, this underperforms against the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF's (XLE) price growth of 78.2% over the same time frame. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, NML's total return jumps up to 205.5% over the last ten years, which reinforces the idea that investors should anticipate the majority of their returns to come from NML's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions.

While NML outperformed XLE over the last ten years, the fund may not be the most efficient option for long-term investors. During unfavorable market conditions, the fund's reliance on net realized gains can threaten its share price. A good example of this can be displayed through the initial market drop during the height of the pandemic in 2020. As markets trended lower, NML struggled to capture net realized gains, which resulted in the fund paying out more than it earned. This exacerbated the rate of NML's share price, causing much larger losses compared to XLE over the same time frame.

Growth Catalyst

With the rise of AI, NML is directly positioned to participate in the expansion. While investors tend to focus on the software side of things, the backbone of the industry is supported by a rise in power demand. Energy and infrastructure companies are directly positioned to service this growing demand over the next few years through the rise of AI data centers.

After assessing NML's top ten holdings, I was able to locate several positions that are already capitalizing on the rising power demands in some capacity. As the positions grow their earnings, the assumption is that NML will be able to capture a greater level of net realized gains.

  • Energy Transfer: Recently raised their 2026 EBITDA guidance, led by pipeline expansions and rising data center demand.
  • Antero Resources (AR): Management confirmed that their production and inventory base positions the business to capture demand opportunities from LNG exports and data centers.
  • MPLX (MPLX): Plans to invest $2.4B in capital projects that will align the business to capture the growing power demand of data centers.

Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI data center market can grow to a value of $810.6B by 2033. This would represent a projected CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 23.9% over the next few years. Even in a scenario where this estimate is off by a 50% margin, this would still indicate the potential for a double-digit upside growth of the AI data center market.

However, I want to emphasize that while NML can participate in this growth, it may not be the best approach for investors that want to maximize total return. If this thesis starts to materialize, NML may need to sell off the upside growth of its positions, which can limit how much the fund's share price will appreciate.

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0701 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.6%. This new payout represents a dividend raise of 20%, which is a sizeable increase from the prior monthly payout of $0.0584 per share. This can be an indication that management is confident in the outlook of its earnings. However, I am a bit cautious about the added pressure that NML now has to produce a higher level of earnings to support these payouts. Assuming NML keeps these distributions the same for the next twelve months, we get an annual payout of $0.8412 per share.

NML reported 2025 earnings of $0.09 per share, which is far from the needed level. However, a scenario where the fund does capture the rising power demands would certainly improve performance. The fund has demonstrated its ability to produce excess earnings in the past. For instance, the fund reported total earnings of $3.01 per share in 2024. However, the increased payouts can cause the NAV to decline further if the fund doesn't generate sufficient earnings.

Furthermore, the latest available section19(a) notice indicates that all of the last payout was supported by return of capital. While return of capital offers some tax efficiency, it also has a direct impact on the NAV. A return of capital distribution is directly paid from a fund's NAV, which means the share price declines by the payout amount. So during a scenario where the share price is already declining, the use of return of capital can exacerbate the pace at which the share price falls.

Ultimately, I would've liked to see the fund demonstrate that it is capable of providing some earnings growth before the dividend was raised. Although I do believe the fund can show positive returns as the power demand grows, I think NML should've waited before raising those payouts. This would have allowed for better NAV growth and capital appreciation for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NML remains a hold at this time. The fund is directly aligned to participate in the expansion of the AI data center market, but there are some tradeoffs to consider. The fund has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions to support the distributions, which can limit capital appreciation through favorable market conditions. Furthermore, the decision to raise dividends seems premature considering the poor earnings for the 2025 period. Therefore, I remain cautious and would like to remain on the sidelines to observe how the fund performs over the next few quarters.

data.title: Harley-Davidson: Losing Its Grip On The Road Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Since my initial article on Harley-Davidson, Inc. (HOG) back in 2023, the company has abandoned its five-year plan, 'The Hardwire,' and has struggled to show improvements in sales and operating margins. At the same time, the company has been working through balance sheet improvements and inventory corrections while the core business continues to face persistent volume declines and weakening demand. Efforts like deleveraging through asset sales and supporting dealers with promotions seemed to have stabilized things in the near term, but they don't address the bigger issues around declining ridership, an aging customer base, and intensifying competition from both premium and lower-cost players. Based on what came out of the Q4 results and the 2026 outlook, I don't think management's current strategy is enough to meaningfully turn the business around. With limited visibility into a return to growth. I maintain a cautious stance on the company's shares.

A Look Back At 2025

Harley-Davidson reported a weak Q4 to close out FY'25 and subsequently saw its share price make 52-week lows as a result of the miss on both the top and bottom lines. On revenues, Q4 sales declined 28% in the quarter as the Harley-Davidson Motor Company segment (HDMC) saw revenues fall 10% to $379 million. In the Harley-Davidson Financial Services segment (HDFS), revenues dropped 59% to $106 million as the finance business shifted to a capital-light model following its strategic transaction. The third segment, LiveWire, has since been spun off to focus exclusively on electric motorcycles.

When looking at what led to the results this quarter, HDFS moved a huge portion of its financing business off of its own books. The main driver of this was a deal with KKR and PIMCO whereby HDFS sold $6 billion in retail finance receivables (the loans people take out to buy bikes). As I'll discuss shortly, this allowed the company to slash its debt position by more than half, from over $6 billion in long-term debt to roughly $2.7 billion.

While the asset sale was responsible for the bulk of the decline, it's no secret that the underlying health of the HDFS business has also been a bit weak. Firstly, the remaining receivables were generating lower yields compared to previous periods, and revenue in the segment was also pressured by lower wholesale finance receivables, reflecting a broader trend of tighter inventory and lower shipments.

As you can probably guess, the number of riders (and therefore customers) has started to go down as Harley-Davidson loses relevance with consumers. For the full year, revenues were $4.5 billion, down 14% from last year, as the HDMC segment's revenue of $3.6 billion fell on 16% lower wholesale motorcycle shipments of approximately 125,000 units and largely flat net pricing.

While I give credit to management for deleveraging the balance sheet and making progress on inventory, which was down 17% versus last year, the declines have been widespread on volumes across all regions. As illustrated in the chart below, North American retail units have fallen 12.6%, with EMEA down 10.9% and APAC down 14.6%. With practically zero bright spots in the portfolio, management's steps on lower-priced sport models, better dealer relationships through revised facility guidelines, and a refocused e-commerce approach to drive showroom traffic have yet to yield results. Indeed, the external pressures show how the brand has lost relevancy with consumers.

Profitability has also come under pressure. Firstly, on HDMC, the company reported a Q4 gross profit loss of $30 million, with the full-year gross margin contracting 380 bps to 24.2%. Gross margins have been hurt by higher tariffs, negative operating leverage from lower volumes, and promotional spending. Further down the income statement, the HDMC operating loss widened to $260 million in the quarter and turned negative $29 million for the full year against $278 million of income previously. As the company spends more on marketing for dealer support, losses have just gotten worse.

On the balance sheet, the HDFS has removed some of the balance sheet concerns here. With operating cash flow now at $569 million, down from $1.06 million, leverage is still high. Cash on hand totaled $3.1 billion and was supported by a $1 billion dividend from HDFS and the planned additional distribution in Q1. With the strong cash position, the company bought back $347 million of shares during the year, including a $200 million accelerated program, but management noted on the call that they're adopting a cautious pace on further buybacks going forward.

Finally, the company's credit rating is now BBB by S&P, and the bond yields on the company's June 2029 bond have tightened to a spread of 123 from 235 in April last year (source: Bloomberg).

A Weak Outlook For Q1'26 And FY'26

Harley-Davidson laid out its 2026 guidance on the Q4'25 earnings call, and it was a pretty grim picture of the reset year in my view. For the core HDMC business, they see the segment producing operating income anywhere from a $40 million loss to a slim $10 million profit, way down from last year's $387 million. Wholesale shipments are expected to come in at 130-135k units (matching flat global retail sales) all while dealing with dealer inventory buildup, $75-105 million in tariff hits, and plans to slow production to fit demand.

To try and offset this, management also said that they would try to target $150 million in cost savings by 2027, which I think is going to be difficult balancing that with marketing spend.

I think they're right to face the music on the inventory mess and last year's double-digit retail drop. Cutting shipments and propping up dealers with promotions is probably the right move to stop the bleeding, and pausing buybacks to preserve cash makes sense. Big-ticket bikes just aren't moving, with buyers sitting on the sidelines.

For me, however, I'm not buying the idea they'll hit breakeven at the top end. Running production below shipments means fixed costs are going to take a big bite, and those tariffs on parts from Thailand for models like the Revolution Max will force either price hikes or squeezed margins right when cheap rivals are gaining ground.

Heading into Q1'26, which is expected to be around May 1 according to Bloomberg, I'm expecting another soft quarter that tests the reset plan laid out in February. Looking at sell-side estimates shown below, it wouldn't surprise me to see wholesale shipments muted given revenue expectations of $1.019 billion, down slightly from the $1.084 billion in Q1'25 last year.

Given that the company has missed analyst expectations in 5 of the last 8 quarters, I think expectations are already muted going in, and I agree with analysts. On the earnings call following the upcoming quarter, I think investors should be focused on Q1 retail breakdowns by region (with a close eye on North America Touring strength and any early green shoots in EMEA or Asia).

As well, since Harley-Davidson relies on a network of 1200+ dealers to engage riders, dealer health is critical. Under prior management, I think dealer economics likely suffered around model exits, poor inventory control, margin actions, and financing pressure. While consolidation is underway (5% decline per year since 2019), it's entirely possible that more may be necessary since relationship repair will take time and traffic remains a problem (source: Bloomberg).

Lastly, on the Q4 conference call, management noted that they will be putting together a "strategic plan announcement" which will hopefully provide more insights on the HDFS transaction and expectations longer term. On this Investor Day specifically, I'll also be keen to see whether management believes they can return to volume growth in the core HDMC business.

Risks To The Business Longer Term

Can retail stabilize? Maybe, but Q1 could easily disappoint since the macro backdrop makes the Harley-Davidson situation much tougher. U.S. motorcycle sales dropped 5.3% last year to about 507k (largely as a result of high financing rates and inflation hitting Gen X wallets). In the chart below, we can also see that industry registration data for new motorcycles is down 6.1% in the United States and 16.4% in Europe. Since tariffs are unlikely to go away under the Trump administration, places like Europe and Asia are still weak. Finally, the silent killer for the business is that Harley's bread-and-butter customers are aging out, and the push for younger riders with entry-level bikes like the Sprint hasn't moved the needle much.

Finally, even though Harley-Davidson is a recognizable name in the space, the industry is full of competition where the company has little pricing power. For example, Polaris' Indian actually grew share last year, and budget players like CFMoto did much better by undercutting on price. Harley's trying with EVs and marketing to millennials, but they're late to the party on adventure bikes and electrics, and used bikes keep cannibalizing new sales.

Valuation And Wrap Up

On valuation, the sell-side is optimistic that Harley-Davidson can return to earnings growth with $1.87 of EPS in 2027 and $2.90 of EPS in 2028. This equates to roughly 12.4x earnings or 8.0x earnings on 2028 numbers. At present, there are 5 'buy' ratings, 10 'hold' ratings, and 2 'sell' ratings. With an average target price of $22.42, however, given the run-up of the shares in the last month, there doesn't look to be much upside from current levels (source: Bloomberg).

Looking past 2026, I just don't see real growth kicking in. Savings might steady things at 135k units, but tariffs could climb higher, demographics keep shrinking the pool, and no quick macro fix is coming. With most sales tied to a weak U.S. market, it's been flat or worse for years. Even with cost cutting of $150 million (which management hasn't exactly said what they're going to be cutting), it won't move the needle on EBITDA of $559 million for the LTM period (source: Bloomberg).

Ultimately, I think Harley-Davidson is at a point where the structural challenges appear to outweigh near-term fixes. Despite management making progress on deleveraging the balance sheet and addressing bloated inventory, I think the company faces a toxic combination of shrinking core demographics, persistent volume declines across all major regions, intensifying competition from both premium rivals and aggressive low-cost players, and meaningful tariff headwinds that will pressure margins at a time when pricing power is virtually nonexistent. Management's 2026 outlook already reflects a sobering reset. With HDMC operating income expected to swing from a healthy profit to roughly breakeven at best, there's a lack of catalysts here to get excited about. At a valuation that prices in some of these risks, I can't knock the stock as a short but would caution investors to stay away for now. As such, I rate shares as a 'Hold.'

data.title: Energy Transfer: Energy Price Fluctuations Are Noisy, Dividend Is The Signal
data.description:
ET stock: Iran conflict and NGL exports

I last analyzed Energy Transfer LP (ET) stock on Mar 4 with an article titled "Energy Transfer: Q4 Could Be A Turning Point." The article served as a review for its FQ4 earnings report (ER) and rated the stock as a hold. Since then, the war with Iran has drastically changed energy prices and the supply-demand dynamics, calling for a revisit to some of the leading stocks in the sector. Against this macroeconomic background, the remainder of this article will concentrate on the sensitivity of ET's profits with respect to energy prices and also the expansion projects ongoing at ET.

To better prime the subsequent discussion, the next two charts show how much oil prices (represented by the Brent Crude Oil Spot Price, BCOSPNK, as shown in the first chart) and natural gas prices (represented by the Henry Hub spot price, HHNGSP, as shown in the second chart) have changed in the past 2 months or so. As seen, the Brent spot price has surged sharply from around $60 in early 2026 to the current level of $127. Natural gas prices have seen an even more erratic surge to above $30 before coming down to the current level of ~$3. For reasons to be detailed next, my key takeaways from these price gyrations are twofold. First, I see the Iran conflict as a positive tailwind for NGL (Natural Gas liquids) exporters such as ET in the near term. Second, for the longer term, I see the ongoing price fluctuations as noises for ET. I consider ET a solid investment idea combining current yield with value regardless of how commodity prices move next.

ET Stock: Iran Conflict's Impact on NGL Exports

In the short term, I believe that the disruptions caused by the Iran conflict will lead to a structural increase in the value of ET (and also other US-based NGL companies in general). The reason behind this notion is not so much about the price increases in energy commodities (more on this in a minute) but more about the shifts in supply-demand dynamics. The conflict has disrupted the supply of a range of energy commodities from the Middle East, and an example is quoted below for Qatar's natural gas flow.

Seeking Alpha News: For the first time since 2008, Qatar has gone five consecutive days without a single LNG export shipment. Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG complex, the world's largest, has shut down following recent Iranian strikes.

With these disruptions, I expect crackers around the world (especially those in Asia with a substantial reliance on feedstock from the Middle East) to rely more on supplies from the US, which should provide a near-term growth catalyst for ET. Additionally, the disruption in the Middle East could also lead to an uptick in US oil production, which could in turn lead to volume growth for ET.

Switching our perspective to the longer term, ET's fee-based model has demonstrated insensitivity to price volatilities of energy commodities. As a reflection, the following two charts illustrate the correlation between ET's dividends (an excellent approximation for its true economic income in my view) with respect to Brent oil prices and also Henry Hub natural gas prices. As seen, the overall pattern in both charts is just random fluctuations with no clear correlation in either direction. To be more explicit, the average correlation coefficients with respect to oil and natural gas prices are essentially zero in the long term (both around -0.06).

Such insensitivity provides a large degree of growth visibility in the long term, in my experience with midstream energy stocks. Their profits largely depend on the infrastructure expansion rather than the random - and often violent, as we are experiencing now- price swings. As a reflection, current consensus projections point to a positive and quite steady long-term earnings trajectory despite the ongoing geopolitical uncertainties. These projections suggest continuous YOY EPS growth in the next 5 years with an 8.9% Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) from FY 2026 through 2030. With the steady climb of EPS, the Forward P/E would compress gradually from 12.32 in 2026 to only 8.04 by 2030.

ET Stock: Expansion Projects and Dividend Growth

As just mentioned, I expect ET's profit growth to be largely determined by its capital expansion projects rather than commodity prices. Under this notion, I do see plenty of capital projections afoot to fuel future growth. In particular, I expect transportation volumes to improve in the Bakken pipeline and the demand for liquefied natural gas (LNG) exports to experience robust growth for the reasons stated above. Meanwhile, ET has eight 10-megawatt natural gas power facilities under construction to enhance grid reliability and address rising domestic energy demand. I expect healthy returns from these facilities with the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence, as they require a massive amount of energy supply to operate. As a reflection of the potential demand for these facilities, ET has started to sign long-term contracts for energy supply directly to the data centers, such as Oracle and CloudBurst (see the news quote below for more details).

Energy Transfer LP (NYSE: ET) today announced that it has entered into a long-term agreement with Denver-based CloudBurst Data Centers, Inc. ("CloudBurst") to provide natural gas to CloudBurst's flagship AI-focused data center development in Central Texas. Gas Supply Expected to Generate Up to 1.2 Gigawatts of Direct Power to AI-Focused Data Center. This represents Energy Transfer's first commercial arrangement to supply natural gas directly to a data center.

To echo my earlier comments about its dividend being a reliable indicator of its true economic growth, ET has been showing an accelerating dividend growth profile in recent years with these growth catalysts. As shown in the table below, over a ten-year horizon, its dividend growth rate stands at a modest 2.07%. However, more recent performance indicates a significant acceleration. The five-year CAGR reached 7.69%, and the three-year CAGR surged to 9.83%, quite consistent with the consensus FWD projection of 8.9% average annual EPS growth in the next 5 years.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

In terms of downside risks, some of the ongoing expansion projects could face execution risks. A key example is the gas pipeline backlog, particularly the greenfield projects in Arizona. The project is susceptible to a range of execution risks in my view, ranging from lengthy regulatory and permitting challenges to cost management issues. The longer the project remains in progress, the more pronounced these risks can become.

In terms of valuation, the stock appears to be trading at a valuation premium judging by its yield and also P/E ratios. Currently, the dividend yield sits at 7.07%, as shown, which is slightly below the historical average of 8.61% in the past 10 years. The valuation premium is even more in terms of FWD P/E ratios (see the last two charts below). The generous yield and the potential of growth acceleration, as discussed, help to mitigate some of the valuation risks. More specifically, the so-called PEGY ratio (as promoted by Peter Lynch for the reasons below) of the stock is currently around 0.77x only with an FWD P/E of 12.9x, a growth projection of 8.9% CAGR, and a current yield of 7.06%.

For dividend stocks, Lynch uses a revised version of the PEG ratio - the PEGY ratio, which is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

All told, this ET revisit is promoted mainly by the changes in the energy market caused by the Iran conflict and also the latest developments of ET's expansion projects. These changes have led me to see ET as a solid investment idea both for the near term and also for the longer term. In the near term, I expect some tailwind from NGL exports. And for the longer term, I see an attractive package combination of value, current income, and growth regardless of where energy prices go next. A key factor that I am monitoring and that could prompt a rating upgrade is valuation. As just mentioned, its yield is currently about 18% below its historical average, and its normalized FWD P/E is about 40% above its historical average. A wider margin of safety in terms of P/E ratios and also yields could lead to a clearer skewness in its return/risk profile.

data.title: GDV: Activist Pressure May Spark Action
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Gabelli Dividend & Income Trust (GDV) closed-end fund has been one of my longer-term holdings. It is a relatively plain vanilla equity-focused fund that primarily invests with a tilt toward more value-oriented holdings. In our previous update, we touched on the potential for the Gabelli Preferred Securities Trust (GPS) spin-off, a preferred-focused CEF. There is only some news on that front, but nothing too definitive yet.

Instead, more interesting recent events are a contested proxy. We had also noted that Saba Capital Management held a position in this fund, and it made this a prime candidate as an activist target given its rather persistent discount to net asset value.

GDV Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.68
  • Discount/Premium: -10.61%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.58%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.54%
  • Leverage: 15.21%
  • Managed Assets: $3.184 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

GDV is described as "a diversified, closed-end management investment company whose objective is to provide a high level of total return." Their investment policy is:

under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its assets in dividend-paying or other income-producing securities. In addition, under normal market conditions, at least 50% of the Fund's assets will consist of dividend-paying equity securities."

Given the fund is leveraged, that increases the overall volatility of the fund and therefore risks to the downside—though the positive is also further potential upside. At the same time, it also means greater expenses. When including the leverage expenses, the total expense ratio comes in at 1.84%.

The fund's leverage is in the form of fixed-rate preferred offerings. Two of these are publicly traded as (GDV.PR.H) and (GDV.PR.K).

Discount Remains Appealing With Some Further Activist Pressure

Along with the broader market, GDV has sold off quite heavily recently but is still up tremendously since our last update. The fund has even outperformed the S&P 500 Index, a relative rarity in the last handful of years when technology and growth have been the primary drivers that have led the market higher.

Though, to be fair, some of the outperformance on a total share price basis has come on the back of the discount narrowing since our last update. Mind you, it still remains at a double-digit discount level, which isn't all that unusual, but it was over 13% previously. That would have put it over its longer-term historical discount level, when it has now tightened up just a hair above that level.

We had previously noted that Saba Capital Management held a position in GDV, and it made sense as a target. This is a fund that has now fairly consistently traded at a relatively wider discount—though off some of the deepest levels we had seen it just a couple of years ago.

Still, Saba owns around a 6.4% stake. This was after reducing their position in January of this year—at one point, the exposure was just over 7%. That made it an interesting proposition for potential discount narrowing in the future. Personally, I planned and plan to hold this fund over the long term, so this was more of an interesting development to watch rather than the sole catalyst to own.

Despite the small reduction by selling some shares from Saba, they have actually more recently launched a contested proxy. This would be in an attempt to get some representation on the Board, which can make pressuring the fund more directly a bit easier.

  • To elect Saba’s nominee, Andre Clemot (the “Nominee”), to serve as a trustee to be voted on by the holders of the Common Shares (as defined below) and the Preferred Shares (as defined below), voting together as a single class, and hold office for a three-year term expiring at the Fund’s 2029 annual meeting of shareholders or until his successor is duly elected and qualified as permitted by law.
  • To elect one trustee to be elected by the holders of the Preferred Shares, voting as a separate class, to serve until the Fund’s 2029 annual meeting of shareholders or until his or her successor is duly elected and qualified (the “Preferred Shares Nominee”).

There is no guarantee they will get this representation, and considering the only 6% or so stake in the fund, this isn't the most aggressive they've been known to be. So there is actually a good chance that the efforts here may be thwarted. However, what makes the case more interesting is that it does appear that Saba is at least still getting a bit more active on this fund in pressuring the management.

To be fair, the management team has been a bit more active recently with raising the distribution. After many years of holding it at $0.11 per month, they've raised it twice in just over the last year.

It still remains at a reasonable 5.88% NAV distribution rate, though these raises have done little to really dent the discount meaningfully.

With the sizeable discount, the distribution rate on the share price even gets bumped up to 6.58%—which I find quite attractive considering I believe that to be a highly sustainable level that could also provide potential capital appreciation going forward. That said, most CEF investors often want to see those 8-10%+ distribution rates, whether they are sustainable over the long term or not.

Preferred Spin-Off?

Further, investors may be wondering about the potential news of the preferred spin-off. That actually would tie into their distribution as well because it would effectively be another distribution bump. Since it would be a spin-off and they stated previously that they plan to keep GDV's distribution the same, investors who hold the newly spun-off preferred fund would be collecting that much more in terms of cash flow into their accounts.

We haven't seen too much news on that front, though, only with their latest annual report.

On February 24, 2025, the Board announced that it had approved the spin off of a newly created closed-end fund (Gabelli Preferred Securities Trust or “GPF”) whose investment objective will be to seek income and capital appreciation by investing in preferred securities. This transaction is subject to shareholder and regulatory approvals.

Now, with the contested proxy popping up from Saba, which they call "Saba Hedge Fund", we have a bit more commentary in their own "white" proxy card. They discuss why investors should ignore Saba's proxy, noting their "short-term goals" driven—which is, to be fair, accurate. Most of Saba's moves are in an effort to reduce the discount quite forcefully through tender offers or ramping up the distributions aggressively.

I admit I tactically invest in some funds to participate in such actions driven by Saba. With GDV, I am overall fairly happy with the management. They tend to be more conservative and deliver a sustainable distribution rate. I know the discount is significant, and I could stand to make a good chunk of change right away. However, I digress.

The main point is that within the commentary, they also provided a very brief but vague update on the potential for the preferred spin-off.

For example, the Board noted that the current Board of Trustees had demonstrated its commitment to seeking to enhance shareholder value by approving a spin-off of a newly created closed-end fund whose investment objective will be to seek income and capital appreciation by investing in preferred securities (“Preferred Trust”) from the Fund. The Board of Trustees believes the spin-off transaction will enhance shareholder value by providing shareholders interests in a separate fund focused primarily on preferred securities which potentially offer attractive opportunities for current income and capital appreciation and may be less sensitive to interest rate fluctuations than other types of income-producing securities and thereby may allow shareholders to more closely align their investment portfolio with their desired exposure to different segments of the market by retaining or disposing of their shares of the Fund and/or Preferred Trust. The spin-off transaction remains subject to regulatory approvals and approval of Fund shareholders, which will be sought at a future meeting of shareholders of the Fund, and this proxy statement is not soliciting any vote or proxy with respect to the spin-off transaction

Interestingly, only noting that it will be sought at a future meeting instead of lumping it into this one. Perhaps that could have made more investors focus on their own proxy card to vote on over Saba's, but only time will tell. At the very least, the Board and management team are still thinking about the spin-off. So that is somewhat promising, considering it was now approved by the Board a year ago.

Conclusion

GDV is a long-term position for me, and we've been watching for potential news on the preferred spin-off, plus keeping an eye on potential moves from Saba Capital. While we have no definitive answer on the timing for the potential preferred spin-off, we do have a contested proxy from Saba. They don't hold an overwhelming position, so getting a nominee elected to the Board seems unlikely.

What it does signal is that Saba is still kicking around and is looking to get more aggressively involved here. In turn, we've seen that alone push the Board and management of some funds to start taking more proactive measures. In this case, that could be the potential for the preferred spin-off or even another distribution increase.

I wouldn't want them to get too aggressive in the payout, but another ~10% bump in the payout could be appreciated by investors. It would be the third raise in around a year or so that could potentially get some more investors' attention, but it would still keep it within a manageable level.
